original_hypothesis.title: FRouter.graduate can be abused by any EXECUTOR_ROLE to drain all assetToken from a pair regardless of token state
original_hypothesis.hypothesis_description: graduate(tokenAddress) transfers the entire assetToken balance from the pair to msg.sender (the executor), with no checks that graduation conditions are met (e.g., token reached threshold, trading disabled, one-time graduation). Any address holding EXECUTOR_ROLE can pull liquidity at any time, potentially breaking the bonding market and diverting assets.
original_hypothesis.investigation_rationale: While role-gated, EXECUTOR_ROLE is often assigned to operational hot wallets/contracts (e.g., Bonding). If compromised or mis-assigned, this becomes an immediate drain. Even without compromise, this is a sharp edge: no invariant enforcement in code.
deployment_assumptions: EXECUTOR_ROLE is assigned to at least one externally reachable contract or operational EOA beyond strictly trusted automation
Pair holds non-trivial assetToken balance (e.g., from trading fees or liquidity)
original_hypothesis.config_evidence: No scripts shown for FRouter role assignments (ADMIN_ROLE/EXECUTOR_ROLE) beyond initialize granting DEFAULT_ADMIN_ROLE only; role provisioning is external/missing in deployment context.

proof_of_signal.pattern: graduate() transfers full assetBalance from pair to msg.sender without checking tokenInfo state or one-time guard

initial_code_evidence.vulnerable_pattern: privileged drain without state machine guard
initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: Liquidity extraction can occur even if token not meant to graduate yet
initial_code_evidence.missing_safeguards: No check of graduation threshold, no 'already graduated' flag, no recipient restriction

analysis_questions: Who receives EXECUTOR_ROLE in production (Bonding, multisig, automation)?
Is graduate intended to only be callable from Bonding._openTradingOnUniswap (and is that enforced anywhere)?
Can an executor repeatedly call graduate to drain newly accumulated asset balances (fees)?
validation_requirements: Review deployment/role assignment transactions for FRouter
Simulate calling graduate pre-threshold and observe pool impact
Check whether FPair/router interactions allow repeated extraction

original_hypothesis.preliminary_attack_scenario: If attacker obtains EXECUTOR_ROLE (misconfig/compromise), they call graduate(token) to pull all asset tokens from the pair to themselves, collapsing the market and stealing assets.

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: Drain of all assetToken liquidity from targeted pairs; market collapse
potential_impact_if_confirmed.affected_users: All traders/holders of tokens in the bonding system
potential_impact_if_confirmed.recovery_possibility: Low; requires admin intervention and recapitalization

original_hypothesis.test_coverage_gap: No tests ensuring graduate is only callable in correct state

technical_investigation_focus.detection_analysis: Track Transfer events from pair tokenB to executor

validation_result.mechanism: Privileged drain without state machine guard
validation_result.impact_summary: Any address with EXECUTOR_ROLE can drain all assetToken liquidity from any pair at any time, leading to a complete loss of funds for the bonding curve.
validation_result.detailed_analysis: The `FRouter` contract acts as the exclusive controller for `FPair` contracts via the `onlyRouter` modifier. It exposes the `graduate(address)` function, which is intended to be called during the bonding curve graduation process to migrate liquidity. However, this function is protected only by the `EXECUTOR_ROLE` and lacks any state machine guards or invariant checks. It does not verify if the token has reached the graduation threshold, nor does it check if the token has already graduated. It simply queries the total `assetToken` balance of the pair and transfers it entirely to `msg.sender`. If `EXECUTOR_ROLE` is granted to an operational wallet, a multisig, or any externally reachable contract other than the strictly trusted `Bonding` contract (or if such a wallet is compromised), the attacker can call `graduate()` on any active pair and instantly steal all accumulated `assetToken` liquidity.
validation_result.code_evidence_assessment: The code evidence directly supports the hypothesis. `FRouter.graduate` retrieves the pair, checks its `assetBalance`, and calls `transferAsset` to send the full balance to `msg.sender`. There are no checks regarding the token's trading state or graduation eligibility.

vulnerable_code_snippets.vulnerability_explanation: The function transfers the entire `assetBalance` of the pair to `msg.sender` without verifying if the token is eligible for graduation or if it has already been graduated. It relies entirely on the `EXECUTOR_ROLE` being perfectly trusted and never misassigned.

execution_path_analysis.vulnerable_code_path: An attacker with `EXECUTOR_ROLE` calls `FRouter.graduate(tokenAddress)`. The router fetches the pair for the given token, reads its entire `assetToken` balance, and instructs the pair to transfer that balance to the attacker (`msg.sender`). The pair executes the transfer, draining the liquidity pool.

deployment_assumptions_checked: EXECUTOR_ROLE is assigned to at least one externally reachable contract or operational EOA beyond strictly trusted automation
config_validation.deployment_evidence: The provided deployment scripts do not explicitly show the assignment of `EXECUTOR_ROLE` for `FRouter`. While it is architecturally required for the `Bonding` contract to hold this role, it is unknown if other operational wallets or EOAs are also granted this role.

validation_result.attack_scenario_validation: If an attacker compromises an operational wallet with `EXECUTOR_ROLE`, or if the role is misconfigured and granted to an untrusted entity, the attacker can iterate through all active tokens and call `graduate(tokenAddress)` for each. This will pull all `assetToken` liquidity from the pairs directly into the attacker's wallet, collapsing the markets and stealing the assets.
validation_result.impact_assessment: High. A compromised or misassigned `EXECUTOR_ROLE` leads to an immediate and complete drain of all `assetToken` liquidity across all active bonding curve pairs. Recovery would be impossible without admin intervention to recapitalize the pools.
recommended_mitigations: Restrict `graduate` to only be callable by the specific `Bonding` contract address rather than a general `EXECUTOR_ROLE`.
Implement a state check in `FRouter` or require a signed authorization from the `Bonding` contract to ensure the token is actually eligible for graduation.
Ensure `msg.sender` is strictly validated as the `Bonding` contract before allowing the transfer of the entire asset balance.
